quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Infinite limit"?

options.text: Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t
μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution
lim(x→c) f(x) = ∞ means f(x) grows without bound as x→c; the limit does not exist as a real number but describes the behavior
A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Infinite limit" is defined as: lim(x→c) f(x) = ∞ means f(x) grows without bound as x→c; the limit does not exist as a real number but describes the behavior. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Vertical asymptote"?

options.text: A vertical line x = c where f(x) → ±∞ as x → c from at least one side
Left: sample at left endpoint; Right: sample at right endpoint; Midpoint: sample at center of each subinterval
= e^(f(x)) · f'(x); the most common chain rule application with exponentials
Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh

quiz_questions.explanation: "Vertical asymptote" is defined as: A vertical line x = c where f(x) → ±∞ as x → c from at least one side. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Finding vertical asymptotes"?

options.text: For f(x) = p(x)/q(x) in simplified form, vertical asymptotes occur where q(x) = 0 and p(x) ≠ 0
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two
Average value has the same units as f(x); the (1/(b-a)) factor divides out the length units
lim[f ± g] = lim f ± lim g; lim[f·g] = lim f · lim g; lim[f/g] = lim f / lim g (if lim g ≠ 0); lim[fⁿ] = (lim f)ⁿ

quiz_questions.explanation: "Finding vertical asymptotes" is defined as: For f(x) = p(x)/q(x) in simplified form, vertical asymptotes occur where q(x) = 0 and p(x) ≠ 0. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Sign analysis"?

options.text: The interval must include both endpoints; on open intervals (a,b), extrema may be approached but not attained
d/dx[f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x); derivative of a sum/difference is the sum/difference of derivatives
Examining the signs of factors near x = c to determine whether f(x) → +∞ or -∞ from each side
To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: 1) find critical points, 2) evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, 3) compare values

quiz_questions.explanation: "Sign analysis" is defined as: Examining the signs of factors near x = c to determine whether f(x) → +∞ or -∞ from each side. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Infinite limit vs. limit at infinity"?

options.text: μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution
When v(t) = 0 AND v(t) changes sign; the particle reverses its direction of travel
f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)
Infinite limit: behavior as x→c (vertical asymptote); limit at infinity: behavior as x→±∞ (horizontal asymptote)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Infinite limit vs. limit at infinity" is defined as: Infinite limit: behavior as x→c (vertical asymptote); limit at infinity: behavior as x→±∞ (horizontal asymptote). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "lim(x→c) f(x) = ∞ means f(x) grows without bound as x→c; the limit does not exist as a real number but describes the behavior"?

options.text: Net change interpretation

Units of derivative

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes is accurate?

options.text: Common scenarios include expanding/contracting shapes, Pythagorean relationships, trigonometric relationships, and similar triangles.
The product rule extends to three or more factors: d/dx[fgh] = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'.
Mean Value Theorem: Applications and Consequences explores the theoretical power of MVT.
Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes describe function behavior near points where values grow without bound.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes?

options.text: An infinite limit lim(x→c) f(x) = ±∞ means f(x) increases or decreases without bound as x approaches c.
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.
The power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n.
Average Value of a Function extends the concept of arithmetic mean to continuous functions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes as covered in this lesson.

The sum, difference, product, quotient, and power laws allow limits to be computed piece by piece.
Combined with the chain rule: d/dx[e^(g(x))] = e^(g(x)) · g'(x) and d/dx[ln(g(x))] = g'(x)/g(x).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes?

options.text: The product rule extends to three or more factors: d/dx[fgh] = f'gh + fg'h + fgh'.

Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).
Implicit Differentiation finds dy/dx when y is not explicitly solved as a function of x.
The sign of the infinite limit depends on the signs of the factors near c.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes?

options.text: Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges
Average temperature: f_avg = (1/24)∫₀²⁴ T(t)dt gives the average temperature over a 24-hour period, used for energy consumption estimates
Radioactive decay: integrating the decay rate dN/dt = -λN gives N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), the fundamental decay equation
Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes?

options.text: Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets
AC electrical circuits: voltage V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt) has derivative V'(t) = V₀ω cos(ωt), the rate of voltage change used in capacitor/inductor analysis
Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Resonance in physics: when driving frequency approaches natural frequency, amplitude of oscillation approaches infinity (vertical asymptote in the response function)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Economics: marginal utility approaching a vertical asymptote at zero consumption, reflecting the extremely high value of the first unit of a scarce resource
Battery charge level: if I(t) is the current flow, the charge Q(x) = ∫₀ˣ I(t)dt represents the accumulated charge at time x
Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Algorithm analysis in computer science: O(n log n) sorting algorithms (merge sort) outperform O(n²) algorithms (bubble sort) for large datasets

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes?

options.text: Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges
Minimizing travel time: finding the optimal route combining different speed zones (e.g., running on sand vs. swimming) using calculus of optimization
Modeling the trajectory of a projectile with air resistance: the implicit equation relating position components requires combined chain rule and implicit differentiation
Electrical engineering: integrating sinusoidal voltage V(t) = V₀sin(ωt) across a capacitor to find charge: Q = ∫V dt = -(V₀/ω)cos(ωt) + C

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes in practice?

options.text: Resonance in physics: when driving frequency approaches natural frequency, amplitude of oscillation approaches infinity (vertical asymptote in the response function)
Radiocarbon dating: ¹⁴C decays with t₁/₂ ≈ 5,730 years; measuring remaining ¹⁴C in organic material determines its age up to ~50,000 years
Temperature modeling: since temperature changes continuously, the IVT guarantees that between any two measured temperatures, every intermediate value was achieved
Numerical integration in computer science: trapezoidal and Simpson's rules are programmed into calculators and software for approximating integrals when antiderivatives don't exist in closed form

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes.
